description:
You are acting as a release-manager automation assistant.  
    All work happens in the directory /home/user/releases.  
    A CSV file called deployments.csv is already present there.  
    1. Read deployments.csv and identify every record whose “status” column equals the literal string “pending”.  
    2. Create a new UTF-8 encoded file /home/user/releases/deploy_queue.json containing ONLY those “pending” rows, represented as an array of JSON objects.  
       • Preserve the original column ordering as JSON keys in this order: id, project, version, environment, status, release_date.  
       • The file must be pretty-printed with two-space indentation and must end with a single trailing newline (ASCII 0x0A).  
    3. Produce a text summary file /home/user/releases/deployment_summary.log that reports simple statistics about deployments.csv.  
       The log MUST have exactly five lines, in the following order (all uppercase key names and “=” signs exactly as shown):  
           TOTAL_RECORDS=<integer>  
           PENDING=<integer>  
           COMPLETED=<integer>  
           ABORTED=<integer>  
           GENERATED_AT=<UTC ISO-8601 timestamp in the form YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ>  
       • Each key/value pair must appear on its own line and the file must finish with a single trailing newline.  
       • The timestamp must be generated at runtime in UTC (use the “Z” suffix).  
    4. Do NOT modify the original CSV file.  
    5. Confirm your work by listing both new files to standard output so the user can see they were created.  

    The automated test suite will verify:  
       • /home/user/releases/deploy_queue.json exists, is valid JSON, and contains exactly the rows whose status was “pending” in deployments.csv.  
       • /home/user/releases/deployment_summary.log exists and matches the strict five-line key=value format given above.  
       • The counts in deployment_summary.log are numerically correct relative to the original CSV.  
       • Both files are UTF-8 encoded and have one—and only one—trailing newline.
truth:
Pre-existing file: /home/user/releases/deployments.csv  
    Contents (6 lines + header, each line ends with LF):  
        id,project,version,environment,status,release_date  
        1,frontend,1.4.2,staging,completed,2024-01-12  
        2,backend,2.3.0,production,pending,2024-03-05  
        3,api,3.1.1,staging,pending,2024-03-11  
        4,billing,4.0.0,production,aborted,2024-02-09  
        5,frontend,1.4.3,production,pending,2024-03-14  
        6,worker,0.9.8,staging,completed,2024-02-28  

    Expected deploy_queue.json (pretty-printed, 2-space indent, trailing newline):  
    [
      {
        "id": "2",
        "project": "backend",
        "version": "2.3.0",
        "environment": "production",
        "status": "pending",
        "release_date": "2024-03-05"
      },
      {
        "id": "3",
        "project": "api",
        "version": "3.1.1",
        "environment": "staging",
        "status": "pending",
        "release_date": "2024-03-11"
      },
      {
        "id": "5",
        "project": "frontend",
        "version": "1.4.3",
        "environment": "production",
        "status": "pending",
        "release_date": "2024-03-14"
      }
    ]
    (followed by single LF)

    Expected numerical values in deployment_summary.log:  
        TOTAL_RECORDS=6  
        PENDING=3  
        COMPLETED=2  
        ABORTED=1  
        GENERATED_AT=<any valid UTC ISO-8601 timestamp checked via regex ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$>  

    Note: automated tests will compare the counts and ensure deploy_queue.json exactly matches rows 2, 3, and 5 above.
